## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

The race to build artificial intelligence infrastructure is the defining capital allocation event of the 2020s.
The leading AI laboratories and their compute partners committed over \$200 billion in 2024 alone, with combined capital expenditure projected to reach \$660--690 billion in 2026 [@palmer2026amazon; @alphabet2025q4; @microsoft2026q2].
OpenAI's Stargate joint venture with SoftBank, Oracle, and MGX alone commits up to \$500 billion in U.S. AI infrastructure through 2029, with an initial deployment of \$100 billion [@openai2025stargate].
These investments are largely irreversible.^[The centrality of compute to AI capability has made it a focal point for governance: governments and firms increasingly use compute allocation, monitoring, and export controls as levers for AI safety and industrial policy; see @sastry2024computing for a comprehensive treatment.]
Yet the fundamental uncertainty driving these decisions---whether and when AI capabilities will reach a transformative threshold---remains profoundly unresolved.

A frontier AI laboratory produces intelligence by combining two forms of compute. *Training compute* is used to optimize a neural network's parameters on massive datasets, yielding a model whose quality scales as a power law of compute invested [@kaplan2020scaling; @altman2025observations]. *Inference compute* runs the trained model to serve predictions and generate revenue. Both activities consume the same scarce hardware, purpose-built GPU clusters housed in multi-billion-dollar data centers, creating an intertemporal allocation problem with no close parallel in traditional capital budgeting.^[In practice, AI infrastructure investment also encompasses substantial expenditure on power generation and transmission---dedicated natural gas plants, long-term power purchase agreements, on-site substations---required to supply the hundreds of megawatts that a single training cluster demands. For tractability, I model all of these complementary investments as a single capital outlay in "compute capacity," with the cost function $I(K) = cK^\gamma$ absorbing the full cost of bringing a unit of capacity online, including its power supply.]

Facilities take 18--24 months to build, and a \$10 billion training cluster cannot be easily repurposed. Executives frame the risk calculus in starkly asymmetric terms. Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai has argued that "the risk of underinvesting is dramatically greater than the risk of overinvesting" [@pichai2024risk], while Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang contends that agentic AI and reasoning require "easily 100x more" compute than anticipated just one year prior [@huang2025scaling]. The prize these investments target is variously called "transformative AI" or "Artificial General Intelligence" (AGI): a step change in model capability sufficient to automate a broad swath of cognitive tasks and generate sustained, qualitatively higher demand for compute [@amodei2024machines]. Executive timeline estimates for this event range from "superintelligence in a few thousand days" [@altman2024intelligence], 2026--2027 [@amodei2024machines], and "AGI in 2026---not a prophecy, but an engineering calculation" [@musk2026agi] to "not with current architectures" [@lecun2025autoregressive]. This dispersion in beliefs is precisely what makes the investment problem so acute: firms must commit irreversible capital based on profoundly different priors about the future.

This paper develops a unified model of irreversible capacity investment under demand uncertainty, combining real options theory [@mcdonald1986value; @dixit1994investment] with regime switching [@guo2005investment], duopoly competition [@grenadier2002option; @huisman2015strategic], endogenous default risk [@leland1994corporate], and diminishing returns calibrated to empirical scaling laws [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training].
The primary contribution is the integrated framework itself: placing a training-inference allocation at the center of a regime-switching investment option with strategic competition and endogenous default generates mechanisms that no subset of the building blocks can produce on its own.
The results fall into three categories.

First, firms choose not only when to invest and how much capacity to install, but how to split that capacity between model training (which builds capabilities for the future) and inference serving (which generates revenue today).
In the current (low) regime, revenue depends on inference capacity through a Tullock contest; in the post-AGI (high) regime, revenue depends on training quality.
This regime-specific competition creates a genuine strategic trade-off absent from standard capacity models: a firm that allocates too much to training sacrifices current revenue and risks default, while a firm that allocates too little sacrifices its competitive position in the transformative AI regime.
I provide analytical characterization of the optimal training fraction, investment trigger, and capacity.
Duopoly preemption changes both timing and training incentives: the leader allocates more to training than the follower because the monopoly phase eliminates the opportunity cost of forgone inference revenue.

Second, the interaction of training allocation with leverage and default risk produces a *faith-based survival* mechanism: training investment raises the effective revenue coefficient $A_{\text{eff}}$ through the expected post-AGI continuation value, lowering the default boundary---but only in parameter regions where this channel dominates current cash-flow weakness, a condition I characterize in closed form for the symmetric duopoly.

Third, I introduce *Dario's dilemma*: the cost to a firm of investing according to a belief about AGI timing that differs from reality. The asymmetry of this trade-off is captured by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei's observation: "I could buy \$1 trillion of compute that starts at the end of 2027. If my revenue is not \$1 trillion dollars, if it's even \$800 billion, there's no force on Earth, there's no hedge on Earth that could stop me from going bankrupt" [@amodei2026cone].
If a firm invests as though AGI is less likely than it truly believes, it invests too late and loses first-mover advantage; if it invests as though AGI is more likely, it overbuilds and risks default.
The training channel amplifies this asymmetry: an overly optimistic firm over-allocates to training, sacrificing the inference revenue it needs to survive.
A stylized calibration to four AI lab archetypes illustrates that the asymmetry is substantial: aggressive overinvestment carries higher downside risk than conservative underinvestment, and the observed capital commitments are consistent with genuinely optimistic beliefs about AI timelines.

The model focuses on duopoly as the core strategic setting.
Frontier AI training competition is highly concentrated: as of 2025, the vast majority of the world's largest training runs are conducted by a handful of laboratories, often backed by or partnered with a single cloud provider (e.g., Anthropic--Amazon, OpenAI--Microsoft) or building proprietary clusters (e.g., xAI's Colossus).
The dominant strategic margin is not atomistic competition but rivalry among a few labs, each making multi-billion-dollar capacity commitments.
Duopoly is the minimal model that captures the key forces---strategic preemption, race-like training allocation incentives, and continuation-value effects under leverage---while maintaining analytical tractability.^[An $N>2$ extension along the lines of @bouis2009multistage may be feasible but would sacrifice the closed-form characterization that makes the duopoly results transparent.]

{{< include _literature.qmd >}}

The paper proceeds as follows.
@sec-model presents the model environment with training-inference allocation, regime switching, the single-firm benchmark, and the duopoly equilibrium with default risk; all analytical results are contained in this section.
@sec-calibration describes the stylized calibration to frontier AI lab archetypes and presents baseline numerical results.
@sec-valuation develops quantitative implications: value decomposition, credit risk, Dario's dilemma, and equity valuation sensitivity.
@sec-discussion considers policy implications, testable predictions, and limitations.
@sec-conclusion concludes.
